identity.title: Paper Bag and Coated and Treated Paper Manufacturing

lens.included_activities: Independent US manufacturers in the approximately $1-10 million normalized EBITDA band repeatedly supplying paper carryout, merchandise, food, pharmacy, and multiwall industrial bags and sacks to external customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Coated or laminated rollstock and packaging paper, surface-coated paperboard, laminated aluminum foil, captive plants, brokers without accountable production, shells, financing vehicles, and firms outside the EBITDA band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring business-to-business supply of standard or custom printed bags and sacks under quotes, purchase orders, releases, and supply agreements, with pricing driven by paper, recycled content, coatings or liners, printing, dimensions, run length, compliance, and freight.
lens.deviation_from_default: NAICS 322220 combines bag and multiwall-sack converting with coated and laminated packaging paper, surface-coated paperboard, and foil-laminate production. The lens retains paper bags and sacks because they share discrete-product converting, printing, customer, and replenishment workflows; rollstock coating and laminating are excluded because their continuous processes, markets, and economics make a single screen incoherent.

a.bridge: BLS reports occupations for all paper manufacturing rather than wage-weighted tasks in narrowed paper-bag and sack firms. The production-heavy mix was translated by exposing estimating, artwork, order entry, scheduling, purchasing, quality and compliance documentation, maintenance support, and customer service while excluding most machine operation and handling; this may understate service work in custom printers and overstate tasks already handled by ERP and prepress systems.
a.rationale: Bag forming, printing, tubing, bottoming, gluing, bundling, material handling, maintenance, and inspection are physical or equipment-led. Repeat specifications, print versions, recycled-content requirements, estimates, job sequencing, and customer administration create a minority AI-addressable task pool.
a.change_evidence: Payroll-weighted task diaries from independent paper-bag and multiwall-sack plants, with current ERP, prepress, vision, scheduling, and line automation documented to avoid counting already automated tasks.
caveats: Occupation data are for NAICS 322, not 322220 or the narrowed lens.
Retail bag and industrial sack plants may have materially different administrative and technical staffing.

rho.rationale: AI can be implemented in specification intake, quoting, artwork comparison, order entry, schedule support, procurement, maintenance retrieval, compliance drafting, and routine service within five years. Legacy systems, line reliability, print accuracy, food-contact controls, recycled-content documentation, exceptions, and human approvals constrain realization.
rho.change_evidence: Audited deployments in bag and sack plants reporting labor hours, quote speed, artwork and specification errors, scrap, downtime, on-time delivery, complaints, and human-review rates before and after implementation.
caveats: No representative AI deployment evidence for the narrowed population was found.
Regulatory change can create new documentation work even as AI reduces time per document.

e.rationale: Independent bag and sack converters ordinarily provide repeat external supply and can be stand-alone transferable businesses. The frozen n also includes excluded coating, laminating, paperboard, and foil operations, while captive ownership, customer concentration, weak equipment, and size-band misclassification further reduce eligibility.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level product and ownership map for all frozen-band 322220 firms, allocating revenue between bags and sacks versus coated or laminated paper, surface-coated board, and foil, with captive status, EBITDA, and customer concentration.
caveats: No source reports the firm share of the retained product group.
The injected n=211 is an ESTIMATE using a broad paper-industry margin that may not fit bag converters.

s5.bridge: Census reports ages for responding employer-business owners across all industries, not paper-bag control transfers. A portion of the 51% age-55-plus share plus ordinary strategic transactions is translated into a five-year qualifying-transfer probability after excluding continued ownership, internal succession, closure, and nontransferable firms; family ownership may create overstatement and packaging consolidation may create understatement.
s5.rationale: Older ownership supports succession pressure and recurring accounts plus converting equipment can support sales. Customer concentration, reinvestment needs, commodity exposure, family succession, and buyer diligence prevent many ownership events from becoming qualifying transfers.
s5.change_evidence: A ten-year census of closed, failed, and withdrawn transactions for independent US paper-bag and multiwall-sack firms in the band, combined with current owner-age and succession-plan interviews.
caveats: Owner-age evidence is all-industry and data year 2018.
The narrowed firms may differ from the overall employer-owner population in age and family ownership.

q.rationale: Plants can retain gains from faster quotes, lower artwork errors, better schedules, less scrap, higher uptime, and responsive service. Paper-cost escalators, bid competition, national retail and food customers, annual cost-down demands, and standardized specifications cause a substantial share to pass through over five years.
q.change_evidence: Customer-contract and quote-history sampling by bag type, showing paper escalators, recycled-content adjustments, renewal cadence, cost-down clauses, win-loss behavior, and margin persistence after measured productivity gains.
caveats: No representative contract dataset was found.
Retention is likely lower for plain standard sacks and higher for short-run custom printed bags.

d5.bridge: BLS projects 1.7% annual real-output growth for all converted paper products rather than bag and sack quantity. California law creates a specific recycled-paper-bag channel but also encourages reusable bags, and FDA's PFAS change affects food-packaging formulations rather than aggregate demand. Applying modest growth can overstate carryout volume if reuse dominates or understate paper substitution from plastic.
d5.rationale: Broader converted-paper output growth and plastic-bag restrictions support paper alternatives. California's 2026 regime permits recycled paper bags while eliminating plastic-film carryout bags and raises recycled-content requirements to 50% in 2028. Offsetting forces include reusable bags, packaging reduction, industrial cyclicality, and material substitution.
d5.change_evidence: US shipment and real-volume series by retail paper bags, food-service bags, merchandise bags, and multiwall sacks, paired with state policy effective dates, retailer bag counts, reusable-bag adoption, and customer end-market forecasts.
caveats: One state's law is not a national demand measure.
BLS covers all of NAICS 3222, and no direct current shipment forecast for the narrowed basket was found.

o.rationale: Bags and sacks are physical containers requiring accountable material selection, printing, forming, sealing, quality control, maintenance, documentation, and delivery. Digital ordering and automated lines reduce labor and transaction work but do not replace the manufacturer; in-sourcing is plausible only for limited simple formats.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of retailers, food-service operators, or industrial users manufacturing paper bags or sacks in-house at scale, or autonomous plants without accountable production and quality management.
caveats: Reusable containers can eliminate some single-use bag quantity, captured in d5.
Distributor or e-commerce self-service may replace sales interactions without replacing the converting operator.

narrative.executive_view: The narrowed paper-bag and sack population has recurring orders, moderate injected labor intensity, and practical AI workflows in quoting, artwork, order handling, scheduling, compliance, and service. Production remains physical, pricing is material-sensitive, and the broad NAICS makes the actual eligible firm pool uncertain.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can parse specifications, draft quotes, compare print revisions, enter and validate orders, assist schedules and purchasing, retrieve maintenance knowledge, prepare recycled-content and food-contact records, and triage service. Forming, printing, gluing, sealing, handling, inspection, and repair remain machine- and human-led.
narrative.value_capture: Less scrap, fewer errors, uptime, and service gains can be retained more readily than transparent administrative reductions. Paper escalators, standardized bids, national accounts, and annual negotiations return part of the benefit to customers.
narrative.firm_availability: The frozen estimate of 211 firms spans bag converting and excluded coating, laminating, surface-coated board, and foil businesses. A product-by-parent map is required before relying on it; broad owner aging supports succession pressure but not an observed transfer rate.
narrative.demand_durability: Paper substitution from plastic and recurring retail, food, and industrial uses support modest growth. Reusable bags, source reduction, industrial cycles, and formulation changes offset that tailwind; California's policy shows both the opportunity for recycled paper and the explicit goal of reducing store-provided bags.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Major gaps are subgroup weights, six-digit occupations, current automation, margin-band accuracy, customer concentration, paper-price terms, direct shipment history, and policy-driven substitution. PFAS and recycled-content changes can require capital, materials development, qualification, and documentation.
narrative.principal_driver: Repeat, specification- and artwork-heavy bag programs create implementable AI opportunities while policy can shift demand from plastic to paper.
narrative.principal_weakness: The broad code mixes incompatible processes, and commodity bidding plus material pass-through limit retained gains in a physical production business.

sources.title: Paper Manufacturing: NAICS 322
sources.supports: BLS reports 2025 paper-manufacturing employment of 87,050 paper-goods machine operators, 17,540 production supervisors, 14,210 industrial-truck operators, and 6,640 production managers, supporting a production-heavy labor mix.

sources.title: NAICS Code 322220: Paper Bag and Coated and Treated Paper Manufacturing
sources.supports: Census index entries show the code combines paper and multiwall bags with laminated or coated combinations of paper, plastic and foil and surface-coated paperboard, supporting the coherence narrowing.

sources.title: Employment and output by industry, 2024-2034
sources.supports: BLS projects converted paper product manufacturing output rising from $99.0 billion in 2024 to $116.9 billion in 2034 in chained 2017 dollars, a 1.7% annual rate, while employment falls 5.4%.

sources.title: California SB-1053: Solid waste, recycled paper bags, standards, carryout bag prohibition
sources.supports: Chaptered California law makes its revised carryout-bag regime operative January 1, 2026, permits qualifying recycled paper bags, eliminates plastic-film carryout bags, and requires recycled paper bags to contain at least 50% postconsumer material from January 1, 2028.

sources.title: Business Owners' Ages
sources.supports: The 2019 Annual Business Survey infographic reports 51% of responding owners of employer businesses were age 55 or older; estimates represent 4.1 million responding owners in data year 2018.

sources.title: Authorized Uses of PFAS in Food Contact Applications
sources.supports: FDA states that in January 2025 it determined 35 food-contact notifications for PFAS grease-proofers applied to paper and paperboard food packaging were no longer effective after abandonment of those uses.
